title: What You Can Trade: Asset Classes
summary: Stocks, ETFs, and crypto — what each instrument actually represents.
concept:
An **asset class** is a family of instruments that behave similarly. **Stocks** (equities) are ownership shares in a company: their value tracks the business and they trade during exchange hours. **ETFs** bundle many assets into one tradable share. **Cryptocurrencies** are bearer digital assets traded on always-open venues — no company, no earnings, just supply, demand, and protocol rules.

QuantGlass focuses on **US equities** and **crypto spot** because both have free public data and transparent pricing. *Spot* means you buy the asset itself; derivatives (futures, options) reference an asset's price with leverage and expiries, and are out of scope here.

Why it matters: the same chart pattern means different things on a stock (driven by earnings, sessions, gaps) than on crypto (24/7, sentiment-heavy, volatile). The engine therefore treats `market_type` as a first-class field on every symbol.

key_terms.definition: A family of instruments with similar behavior and rules — equities, crypto, bonds, commodities.
A share of ownership in a company.
Exchange-traded fund: a basket of assets trading as one share.
Buying or selling the asset itself for immediate settlement.
A contract whose value references another asset's price — out of QuantGlass scope.

exercise.question: Which statement about crypto spot markets is accurate?
options: They close at 16:00 New York time like stock exchanges.
You own the asset itself, and trading continues around the clock.
Every crypto trade is a leveraged futures contract.
Prices are set once per day by the protocol.
exercise.explanation: Spot crypto trades the asset itself on venues that never close. Stock exchanges have sessions; futures and leverage are derivative features, not spot.

live_apply.cta: Open the Watchlist and note which symbols are crypto and which are stocks — the market type tag drives how the engine treats each.

title: Exchanges and Venues
summary: Where trades actually happen, and why the venue affects your price.
concept:
An **exchange** is a venue that matches buyers and sellers under published rules. US stocks trade on regulated exchanges (NYSE, Nasdaq) plus dozens of alternative venues, all stitched together by regulation into one national best price. Crypto is different: each exchange (Coinbase, Kraken, Binance) is its **own island** with its own order book, so the *same* coin can trade at slightly different prices on different venues at the same moment.

That is why QuantGlass lets you choose **provider routes** — primary, secondary, and fallback data sources per market. When Coinbase data is unavailable the engine can fall back to Kraken; the candles will be nearly, but not exactly, identical.

Rule of thumb: always know *which venue's price* you are looking at. A signal computed on one venue's candles applies to that venue's prices.

key_terms.definition: A rule-governed venue matching buyers and sellers.
The exchange's live list of resting buy and sell orders.
The same asset trading on multiple venues at slightly different prices.
QuantGlass's primary/secondary/fallback choice of data venue per market.

exercise.question: BTC shows 67,950 on one exchange and 68,010 on another at the same time. Why?
options: One of the two prices must be an error.
Each crypto exchange has its own order book, so prices can differ slightly between venues.
Bitcoin's protocol sets a different price per country.
One exchange is showing yesterday's close.
exercise.explanation: Crypto venues are independent order books. Small price differences are normal and are kept in line by arbitrage traders.

live_apply.cta: Open Settings → Providers and find the crypto route: which venue is primary, and which is the fallback?

title: Sessions and the Market Clock
summary: When markets are open, why it matters, and how crypto differs.
concept:
US stock exchanges run a **regular session** (09:30–16:00 New York time) book-ended by thinner pre-market and after-hours sessions. Volume and liquidity concentrate near the **open** and the **close**; lunchtime is typically quiet. Crypto never closes — but it still has rhythm: activity clusters around US and Asian waking hours, and weekends are thinner.

Sessions matter to a signal reader for one big reason: **candles need to close**. A 1-day stock candle closes at 16:00 New York; a 1-hour crypto candle closes at the top of each hour. QuantGlass evaluates **closed candles only** — a pattern that appears mid-candle can vanish before the close, so acting on an unfinished candle is acting on noise.

The signal's freshness badge tells you how old the last *closed* candle is, so you always know which clock you are on.

key_terms.definition: The main trading hours of an exchange — 09:30–16:00 New York for US stocks.
Thinner stock sessions around the regular one, with wider spreads.
The moment a timeframe's bar is finalized — the only price the engine evaluates.
How old the latest closed candle is, shown on every QuantGlass signal.

exercise.question: Why does QuantGlass wait for a candle to close before evaluating a setup?
options: Closed candles download faster.
Until the close, the candle's final high, low, and close can still change — mid-candle patterns can vanish.
Exchanges only publish data once per day.
Open candles are illegal to analyze.
exercise.explanation: A candle is only a fact after it closes. The engine's closed-candle rule prevents acting on patterns that may not survive the bar.

live_apply.cta: Open Signals and check a freshness badge: how old is the last closed candle behind the newest signal?

title: The Order Book, Bid, Ask, and Spread
summary: Every price you see is really two prices.
concept:
At any moment an exchange holds resting orders: the highest price a buyer will pay is the **bid**; the lowest price a seller will accept is the **ask**. The gap between them is the **spread**. A 'price' quote is usually the last trade, which bounced between those two walls.

The spread is your first, invisible cost: buy at the ask, and you are instantly down one spread if you were to sell back at the bid. Liquid markets (major stocks, BTC, ETH) keep spreads at a few hundredths of a percent; thin markets can have spreads of 1% or more — large enough to erase a small edge entirely.

QuantGlass's backtests charge a **slippage** allowance on every simulated fill precisely because real fills happen at the spread's edges, not at the midpoint shown on a chart.

key_terms.definition: The highest resting buy price.
The lowest resting sell price.
Ask minus bid — the instant round-trip cost of crossing the book.
The most recent trade — it bounces between bid and ask.
The difference between the chart price and your actual fill price.

exercise.question: A coin shows bid 99.95 / ask 100.05. You buy at market and immediately sell at market. Ignoring fees, what happens?
options: You break exactly even.
You lose about 0.10 — you bought at the ask and sold at the bid.
You gain the spread as a rebate.
Both orders fill at 100.00, the midpoint.
exercise.explanation: Market orders cross the spread: buy at 100.05, sell at 99.95 — a 0.10 loss. The spread is a real cost the chart's single price line hides.

live_apply.cta: Open a symbol and imagine the candle's close as a bid/ask pair around it — the engine's slippage assumption models exactly that gap.

visuals.title: Press market buy and watch your urgency eat the book.

title: Order Types: Market and Limit
summary: The two fundamental orders, and what each trades away.
concept:
A **market order** says *fill me now at the best available price* — execution is certain, price is not. A **limit order** says *fill me only at my price or better* — price is certain, execution is not. Everything fancier (stop-loss, stop-limit, take-profit) is built from these two primitives plus a trigger.

A **stop-loss** is a resting trigger: when price touches your stop level, it fires a market order to exit. That is exactly the geometry every QuantGlass signal shows — an **entry zone** (where a limit-style entry makes sense), a **stop-loss** level (your exit trigger if wrong), and a **take-profit ladder** (staged limit exits if right).

QuantGlass paper trading fills intents at the next available price — modeling a simple market order — so you experience execution uncertainty honestly rather than getting perfect textbook fills.

key_terms.definition: Execute immediately at the best available price — certain fill, uncertain price.
Execute only at a chosen price or better — certain price, uncertain fill.
A trigger level that fires an exit order when price touches it.
A resting exit at a target price — QuantGlass shows a three-rung ladder.

exercise.question: You must exit a losing position right now in a fast-falling market. Which order guarantees you get out?
options: A limit order at your entry price.
A market order — you accept whatever price the book offers.
A take-profit order above the market.
No order type can ever exit a position.
exercise.explanation: Only a market order guarantees execution, at the cost of price certainty. A limit at your entry would likely never fill in a falling market.

live_apply.cta: Open a signal and identify its three order-type components: the entry zone, the stop-loss trigger, and the take-profit ladder.

visuals.title: Every fancy order is built from these.

steps.text: Exit if price hits a level?

common_mistakes: Using market orders in thin books and being surprised by the fill price. The guarantee is execution, never price.
Setting a stop-limit and assuming it protects like a stop. In a fast market the limit leg can go unfilled and the position rides through.
Placing stops at obvious round numbers where everyone else's sit - the exact pools that get swept.

title: Liquidity and Volume
summary: Why size moves price, and how volume validates a move.
concept:
**Liquidity** is how much you can trade without moving the price; **volume** is how much *was* traded. They are related but not identical: volume is the receipt, liquidity is the capacity.

When a market is deep (thick order book), even large orders barely move price. When it is thin, a modest market order eats through several price levels at once — that is slippage in action. Liquidity also dries up at night, on weekends (crypto), and around news.

For a signal reader, volume is a **confirmation tool**: a breakout on twice the recent average volume means real participation; the same breakout on dead volume is suspect. QuantGlass encodes this as the **volume ratio** (current volume ÷ recent average) and folds it into a signal's `volume_confirmation` score — one of the confidence inputs you can inspect on every signal.

key_terms.definition: How much can be traded without moving the price.
The amount actually traded in a period.
Current bar's volume divided by the recent average — QuantGlass's participation gauge.
Low liquidity: small orders cause outsized price moves.

exercise.question: A breakout candle prints on volume 2.3× its recent average. What does that ratio tell you?
options: Nothing — volume is unrelated to price moves.
Participation is unusually high, which strengthens the breakout's credibility.
The exchange is malfunctioning.
Liquidity has disappeared from the book.
exercise.explanation: Volume ratio compares the bar to its own recent baseline. Breakouts on elevated participation are historically more reliable than dead-volume moves.

live_apply.cta: Open a symbol's detail and find the volume metrics — compare the latest bar's volume against the recent baseline.

visuals.title: Volume ratio is the engine's participation gauge.

title: Fees and Slippage: The Round-Trip Cost
summary: Every trade pays twice — compute the true cost before counting any edge.
concept:
Two costs hit every single trade. **Fees** are explicit: exchanges charge a percentage per side (crypto taker fees are commonly around 0.10%; US equity commissions are near zero but other frictions remain). **Slippage** is implicit: the gap between the chart price and your real fill, born from the spread and book depth.

Both are paid **twice** — once entering, once exiting. QuantGlass's default cost model assumes, per side, **0.10% fee + 0.05% slippage for crypto** and **0.02% + 0.01% for stocks**, and the backtester deducts the full round trip from every simulated trade. A strategy that looks profitable before costs and unprofitable after them has no edge — it has an illusion.

Example: a 10,000 crypto position pays (0.10% + 0.05%) × 2 = **0.30%** round trip — 30 before the trade has gone anywhere.

key_terms.definition: The explicit percentage an exchange or broker charges per side.
The implicit cost between chart price and actual fill.
Entry plus exit — costs are paid on both.
A simulation that deducts fees and slippage from every trade, as QuantGlass does by default.

exercise.question: Using QuantGlass's crypto defaults (0.10% fee + 0.05% slippage per side), what is the total round-trip cost in dollars on a 10,000 position?
exercise.explanation: (0.0010 + 0.0005) × 2 = 0.0030; 10,000 × 0.0030 = **30**. The engine deducts exactly this from every simulated crypto round trip.

live_apply.cta: Open Backtesting and find the fees/slippage assumptions on a preset — they match the defaults this lesson used.

visuals.title: Round-trip cost per side, engine defaults.

title: Who Is on the Other Side
summary: Market makers, institutions, algos, and you.
concept:
Every fill has a counterparty. **Market makers** quote both bid and ask all day, earning the spread while providing liquidity. **Institutions** (funds, treasuries) move size slowly to avoid impact — their footprints are sustained volume trends. **Algorithmic traders** range from microsecond arbitrage to slow systematic strategies, and account for most modern volume. **Retail traders** — you — are the smallest and slowest participants.

This is not a reason for despair; it is a reason for **discipline**. A retail trader's edge is never speed. It is selectivity (trade only clean setups), patience (let the closed candle confirm), risk control (size from the stop, not from conviction), and honesty about statistics — exactly the four things QuantGlass's engine enforces mechanically.

Knowing who you trade against tells you which games not to play: you cannot out-react a co-located algo, but you can out-wait it.

key_terms.definition: A participant quoting both sides continuously, earning the spread.
Large, slow orders worked over time to limit market impact.
Automated strategies — the majority of modern volume.
Selectivity, patience, and risk control — not speed.

exercise.question: Which of these is a realistic edge for a retail trader?
options: Reacting to news faster than co-located algorithms.
Being selective, waiting for closed-candle confirmation, and sizing every trade from its stop.
Trading more frequently than market makers.
Getting better fees than institutions.
exercise.explanation: Speed, frequency, and fee games belong to professionals. Selectivity, patience, and mechanical risk control are the edges that survive at retail scale.

live_apply.cta: Revisit the position-sizing lesson in Risk & Position Sizing — it is the most concrete expression of the retail edge.

visuals.title: Know which games not to play.

title: Crypto vs Equities Inside QuantGlass
summary: Same engine, two very different markets.
concept:
QuantGlass runs one deterministic engine over two market types, and the differences are encoded rather than ignored.

**Hours**: crypto candles flow 24/7; stock candles only exist for exchange sessions, so a 1-day stock candle is a session, not a calendar day. **Volatility**: crypto's typical ATR as a share of price runs far higher, which is why stops are ATR-multiples instead of fixed percentages. **Costs**: the default round-trip model charges crypto 0.30% and stocks 0.06% — the backtester applies whichever matches the symbol's `market_type`. **Symbols**: crypto pairs are quoted like `BTCUSD` (asset/quote currency), stocks are exchange tickers like `AAPL`.

Practical consequence: never copy position sizes or stop distances across market types. The engine's regime and volatility logic adapts automatically; your expectations should too.

key_terms.definition: QuantGlass's first-class crypto/stocks distinction on every symbol.
A stop sized as a multiple of current volatility, not a fixed percent.
A crypto symbol quoting one asset in another, e.g. BTC in USD.
A stock daily candle covering exchange hours only.

exercise.question: Why does QuantGlass size stops as ATR multiples instead of a fixed percentage for all symbols?
options: Fixed percentages are illegal.
Volatility differs enormously across symbols and market types — an ATR multiple adapts the stop to each market's actual movement.
ATR is easier to compute than a percentage.
Stops are decorative.
exercise.explanation: A 2% stop might be huge for a quiet stock and tiny for a volatile coin. ATR multiples normalize the stop to how much the instrument actually moves.

live_apply.cta: Compare a crypto and a stock symbol's recent candle ranges — notice how differently they move, then find that difference expressed in their ATR values.

title: From Ticks to Candles: How OHLCV Is Made
summary: Where the engine's raw material comes from.
concept:
Markets produce a stream of individual trades — **ticks**. A candle compresses all ticks inside a time window into five numbers: **O**pen (first trade), **H**igh, **L**ow, **C**lose (last trade), and **V**olume (total traded). Change the window and you change the story: the same hour is one 1h candle, four 15m candles, or a quarter of a 4h candle.

QuantGlass ingests OHLCV at fixed timeframes (**15m, 1h, 4h, 1d**), stores them locally, and stamps every series with its **source** venue and ingestion time. Each candle also carries its `open_time_utc` — the engine's freshness math derives the close time from it plus the timeframe length.

Two data rules keep analysis honest: candles are **immutable once closed** (a changed history is a provider bug, and the integrity layer flags it), and **every number you see traces to a venue** — no blended, untraceable prices.

key_terms.definition: A single executed trade.
Open, High, Low, Close, Volume — the five numbers summarizing a window of ticks.
The window length of each candle — 15m, 1h, 4h, or 1d in QuantGlass.
Checks that closed candles never mutate and gaps are detected.

exercise.question: A 1-hour candle shows O=100, H=104, L=99, C=103. What is the one thing you can NOT conclude from it?
options: The first trade of the hour was at 100.
Price touched 104 at some point during the hour.
The exact path price took between those levels.
The last trade of the hour was at 103.
exercise.explanation: A candle is a summary: it preserves the extremes and endpoints but destroys the path. Price could have hit the low before the high or vice versa — the candle cannot say.

live_apply.cta: Switch a chart between 15m and 1h — watch four small candles fuse into one larger one carrying the same OHLCV information.

visuals.title: Compression destroys the path but keeps the extremes.
Drag the four numbers; replay the compression.

title: Reading a Candlestick
summary: Understand the four price values that every candle encodes.
concept:
A **candlestick** encodes four prices into a single bar: the **Open** (where the period started), the **High** and **Low** (the extreme prices reached), and the **Close** (where the period ended).  The rectangular *body* spans Open→Close.  A *green/bullish* candle means Close > Open; a *red/bearish* candle means Close < Open.  The thin lines above and below the body are called **wicks** (or shadows) and show the High and Low.

**Volume** — the number of units traded during the period — appears below the chart.  High volume on a strong close signals conviction; high volume on a reversal signals exhaustion.

key_terms.definition: Price at the very first trade of the period.
Highest traded price during the period.
Lowest traded price during the period.
Price at the very last trade of the period.
The rectangle between Open and Close.
The thin line extending beyond the body to the High or Low.
Number of units traded during the period.

exercise.question: A candle opens at 100, reaches a high of 110, falls to 95, and closes at 107.  What does the upper wick represent?
options: The price traveled from 107 to 110 and was rejected back down.
The average price for the period.
The opening price of the next candle.
The total volume transacted.
exercise.explanation: The **upper wick** represents the distance between the Close (107) and the High (110).  Price reached 110 but buyers couldn't sustain it — sellers pushed it back to 107 by the close.  A long upper wick on a bullish candle is often a warning of overhead supply.

live_apply.cta: Open the Dashboard, hover any candle on the chart, and verify the O/H/L/C values in the tooltip.

visuals.title: The body's color is the period's verdict.

common_mistakes: Reading a single candle as a signal. One candle is one auction; only its context (trend, level, volume) gives it meaning.
Memorizing pattern names instead of the buying/selling pressure they encode. The label is trivia; the pressure is the information.
Ignoring the timeframe. The same 'hammer' on a 1-minute chart and a weekly chart carry completely different weight.

title: What Makes a Trend
summary: Learn how higher highs and lower lows define direction.
concept:
A **trend** is a series of price swings moving predominantly in one direction.

- **Uptrend**: price makes successively **Higher Highs (HH)** and **Higher Lows (HL)**.  Each pullback sets a higher floor than the last.
- **Downtrend**: price makes successively **Lower Highs (LH)** and **Lower Lows (LL)**.
- **Range / Sideways**: highs and lows oscillate within a band with no sustained direction.

QuantGlass identifies trend using the relationship between the **EMA-21** (short-term average) and the **SMA-50** (medium-term average).  When `Close > EMA21 > SMA50`, the engine classifies the market as bullish trend.  When `Close < EMA21 < SMA50`, it is bearish.

key_terms.definition: Each successive swing peak is above the prior peak.
Each successive pullback bottom is above the prior bottom.
21-period Exponential Moving Average — weights recent closes more.
50-period Simple Moving Average — equal weight across all 50 closes.

exercise.question: Price closes at 250, EMA-21 is at 245, SMA-50 is at 240.  What trend state does QuantGlass assign?
options: Bearish — price is below both averages.
Bullish — Close > EMA21 > SMA50.
Ranging — the averages are too close together.
Volatile — cannot determine direction.
exercise.explanation: When `Close (250) > EMA21 (245) > SMA50 (240)` the stack is perfectly bullish.  The signal engine uses this alignment to gate BUY_ZONE setups in the trend-pullback family.

live_apply.cta: Open Signals, click any BUY_ZONE signal, and look at the Reasons section — find the trend alignment reason.

visuals.title: The staircase that defines an uptrend.

title: Support & Resistance Basics
summary: Discover the price levels where supply and demand repeatedly clash.
concept:
**Support** is a price level where falling price repeatedly bounces — buyers outweigh sellers there.  **Resistance** is a level where rising price repeatedly stalls — sellers outweigh buyers.

These levels form because traders remember past turning points.  A broken resistance level often becomes the new support (*polarity flip*).  QuantGlass uses the **Donchian Channel** (20-period highest high / lowest low) to identify recent swing extremes that act as natural S/R.

key_terms.definition: Price floor — buyers step in to arrest falling price.
Price ceiling — sellers step in to arrest rising price.
Broken resistance becomes support after a confirmed breakout.
The highest high and lowest low over N periods.

exercise.question: Price breaks above a resistance level at $500 on heavy volume, then pulls back to $500.  What is $500 now?
options: Still resistance — it hasn't proved itself yet.
Support — the polarity has flipped after a confirmed breakout.
The fair-value price — mean reversion target.
A stop-loss level.
exercise.explanation: After a confirmed breakout on volume, the prior resistance at $500 becomes support.  This is the *polarity flip*.  The breakout-retest-continuation setup in QuantGlass specifically looks for price returning to the Donchian breakout level before re-entering.

live_apply.cta: Find a WATCH signal with setup type 'breakout_retest_continuation' — the entry zone is near the flipped level.

common_mistakes: Treating levels as exact prices instead of zones. Markets respect areas; precise lines get wicked through constantly.
Drawing a level through every touch until the chart is unusable. Two or three clean, widely-respected zones beat twelve noisy lines.
Forgetting that broken support tends to become resistance. Old levels do not disappear when breached - they switch sides.

title: Volume as Confirmation
summary: Learn why volume gives price moves credibility.
concept:
**Volume** is the number of units traded.  A price move *with* strong volume is more likely to sustain than a move on thin volume.  Volume confirms that enough participants agree with the direction.

QuantGlass computes a **volume ratio** — current bar volume divided by the 20-period average.  A ratio ≥ 1.75 earns full `volume_confirmation` credit in the confidence formula.  Breakout entries (Donchian break) require volume_ratio ≥ 1.0 as a minimum gate.

**Divergence**: price rises but volume falls → weak move, possible exhaustion.  Price falls on high volume → institutional distribution, bearish signal.

key_terms.definition: Current bar volume ÷ 20-period average volume.
A 0–1 score: how much volume supports the current move.
Price and volume moving in opposite directions — weakens the move.

exercise.question: A bullish breakout candle has volume 3× the 20-period average.  What does this tell you?
options: It's a false breakout — high volume means exhaustion.
The move has strong institutional participation and is more likely to sustain.
Volume is irrelevant for breakouts.
You should wait for volume to decrease before entering.
exercise.explanation: High volume on a breakout confirms that many participants are in agreement.  Institutions cannot hide their activity — they leave a volume footprint.  QuantGlass's volume_confirmation score reaches 1.0 when volume_ratio ≥ 1.75.

live_apply.cta: Open a signal detail drawer and inspect the confidence_basis.volume_confirmation value.

title: Timeframes: One Market, Many Lenses
summary: The same price action looks different at 15m, 1h, 4h, and 1d.
concept:
A **timeframe** is the window each candle summarizes. Lower timeframes (15m) show fine detail and more noise; higher timeframes (1d) show structure and less noise. Neither is 'more true' — they are different compressions of the same tick stream.

Two practical rules. First, **trend is timeframe-relative**: a 15m downtrend can live inside a 1h uptrend inside a 1d range. Always name the timeframe when you name a trend. Second, **signals inherit their timeframe**: a QuantGlass signal generated on 1h candles has 1h-scale stops, targets, and holding times — judging it against a 1d chart misreads it.

The engine's setups even consult a slower lens internally: the higher-timeframe slope check compares the 50-bar baseline now versus 10 bars ago, rewarding setups aligned with the larger drift.

key_terms.definition: The window length each candle summarizes.
Short-horizon fluctuation that disappears at higher compression.
Agreement between a setup's timeframe and the slower trend — a confluence input in QuantGlass.
How long a position is expected to stay open — scales with the timeframe.

exercise.question: A symbol is falling on the 15m chart but rising on the 1d chart. Which statement is correct?
options: The data feed is broken.
Both are true: trend is relative to the timeframe you measure it on.
The 15m chart always overrides the 1d chart.
The symbol has no trend.
exercise.explanation: Trends nest. A short-lens pullback inside a long-lens uptrend is the normal anatomy of trending markets — and the basis of pullback setups.

live_apply.cta: Flip a chart through 15m → 1h → 4h → 1d and write down the trend you would call at each lens.

title: Swings, Trendlines, and Structure
summary: Higher highs, higher lows, and the skeleton under every chart.
concept:
Price moves in **swings** — pushes and pullbacks. Mark the turning points and you get the market's skeleton: an **uptrend** is a staircase of higher highs (HH) and higher lows (HL); a **downtrend** is lower highs (LH) and lower lows (LL); a **range** is overlapping swings with no staircase.

A **trendline** connects successive swing lows (in an uptrend) or swing highs (in a downtrend). It is not magic — it is a visual summary of the staircase's pace. When a staircase breaks (price takes out the last HL in an uptrend), the structure is damaged, and structure damage is the chartist's first warning, long before any indicator turns.

QuantGlass's signal reasons often cite recent swing extremes — the invalidation anchor on a long setup is typically the recent swing low, because that is where the staircase logic itself fails.

key_terms.definition: A local turning point in price.
The staircase patterns defining up- and downtrends.
A line through successive swing points summarizing trend pace.
Price violating the most recent swing of the prevailing staircase.

exercise.question: In an uptrend defined by higher highs and higher lows, which single event most directly damages the trend's structure?
options: A red candle of any size.
Price breaking below the most recent higher low.
Volume declining for one bar.
A weekend gap.
exercise.explanation: The staircase survives red candles and quiet volume. It is defined by its swing lows — losing the latest higher low is what breaks the pattern.

live_apply.cta: On a symbol's chart, mark the last three swing lows mentally — then find the signal's invalidation level and see how it relates to them.

title: Gaps, Wicks, and What They Reveal
summary: The chart's punctuation marks: rejection, surprise, and absence.
concept:
A **wick** (shadow) is the part of a candle outside its body: price went there and was pushed back within the same bar. A long upper wick on a rally candle means sellers absorbed the push — **rejection**. A long lower wick after a decline means buyers stepped in. Wicks at key levels (support, resistance, moving averages) are how rejection setups announce themselves.

A **gap** is a jump between one candle's close and the next candle's open. Stock charts gap routinely — news lands while the exchange is closed. Crypto, trading 24/7, rarely gaps; a crypto 'gap' usually means a data outage, which QuantGlass's integrity checks flag rather than silently bridge.

Reading rule: a wick is information about *within-bar* battle; a gap is information about *between-bar* surprise. Neither is a signal alone — both are context that strengthens or weakens a setup.

key_terms.definition: The candle's range outside its body — visited but rejected prices.
A push to a level that closed back away from it.
Open differing from prior close — common in stocks, anomalous in crypto.
A missing-candle artifact the integrity layer detects.

exercise.question: A crypto 1h series shows a large gap between one candle's close and the next candle's open. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Crypto exchanges close overnight like stock markets.
A data outage — continuous markets rarely produce true price gaps, so QuantGlass flags missing candles.
The protocol reset the price.
Gaps are impossible to represent in OHLCV.
exercise.explanation: Crypto trades around the clock, so consecutive candles normally touch. A gap usually marks missing data — an integrity event, not a market event.

live_apply.cta: Find a candle with a pronounced wick near a moving average — that geometry is the raw material of the engine's rejection setups.

title: Reading Levels Like the Engine Does
summary: From hand-drawn lines to reproducible levels.
concept:
Classic support/resistance is drawn by eye — useful, but irreproducible: two traders draw two different lines. QuantGlass prefers **computable levels** so every level you see can be recomputed and audited.

The engine's level vocabulary: **recent extremes** (the highest high / lowest low of the last ~20 bars — the Donchian channel), **moving averages** as dynamic support (the 21-EMA pullback zone in trend setups), and **volatility envelopes** (Keltner: EMA ± a multiple of ATR; Bollinger: mean ± a multiple of standard deviation). Each is a precise formula over closed candles — no artistic license.

When a signal says 'pullback into the EMA zone' or anchors invalidation 'near the recent swing low', those phrases map one-to-one onto these computed levels. Hand-drawn lines remain a fine thinking tool; the engine just refuses to *trade* anything it cannot recompute.

key_terms.definition: A support/resistance level derived from a formula, not a hand drawing.
Highest high and lowest low over N bars — the engine's recent-extreme level.
A moving average acting as a rising/falling level, e.g. the 21-EMA.
Bands around a center line sized by ATR (Keltner) or standard deviation (Bollinger).

exercise.question: Why does QuantGlass anchor its levels to formulas (Donchian, EMA, ATR bands) instead of hand-drawn lines?
options: Formulas always predict the future correctly.
Computed levels are reproducible and auditable — every level on screen can be recomputed from the same candles.
Drawing tools are patented.
Hand-drawn lines are always wrong.
exercise.explanation: The point is honesty, not prophecy: a formula-derived level can be verified by anyone with the same data, which is the standard the whole engine holds itself to.

live_apply.cta: Toggle the band overlays on a chart and watch how price interacts with the computed envelope edges.

title: Reading Your First Signal
summary: Decode every field in a QuantGlass signal output.
concept:
A QuantGlass signal contains:

- **Signal type**: BUY_ZONE / SELL / HOLD / WAIT / WATCH
- **Entry zone**: a price range [low, high] to enter within
- **Stop-loss**: price where the trade idea is proven wrong
- **Take-profit [TP1, TP2, TP3]**: three exit rungs (50 / 30 / 20% of position)
- **Risk:Reward (R:R)**: how many R you earn if TP2 is reached
- **Confidence (0–100)**: calibrated score — not a win probability
- **Confidence basis**: the sub-scores that drove the overall confidence
- **Reasons**: plain-English explanation of why this setup was identified
- **Invalidation**: the condition that cancels this setup

**Important**: Confidence ≠ win rate.  A 70-confidence signal is NOT '70% likely to win'.  It means the engine has relatively strong evidence *at the time of the scan*.  Always check `out_of_sample_validated` — if False, the backtest hasn't cleared statistical scrutiny.

key_terms.definition: The price range [low, high] considered acceptable to enter.
Price level where the setup is invalidated; maximum acceptable loss.
Three scaled exit prices (TP1=50%, TP2=30%, TP3=20% of position).
Reward ÷ Risk — a 2.0 R:R means the potential gain is 2× the risk.
True when the OOS backtest slice has ≥ min_sample trades with positive expectancy.

exercise.question: A BUY_ZONE signal shows entry 100–102, stop 96, TP2 = 108.  What is the approximate R:R to TP2?
options: R:R ≈ 1.0 — reward equals risk.
R:R ≈ 1.5 — reward is 50% more than risk.
R:R ≈ 2.0 — reward is double the risk.
R:R ≈ 0.5 — risk is double the reward.
exercise.explanation: Entry midpoint ≈ 101, Stop = 96 → Risk = 5.  TP2 = 108 → Reward = 7.  R:R = 7/5 = 1.4 ≈ 1.5.  QuantGlass targets a minimum 1.5–2.4× R:R depending on setup type.

live_apply.cta: Click any BUY_ZONE signal and locate entry_zone, stop_loss, take_profit, and risk_reward in the detail drawer.

title: Anatomy of a QuantGlass Signal
summary: Every field on the signal card, and why it exists.
concept:
A QuantGlass signal is a structured claim, not a tip. Its parts: the **state** (BUY_ZONE, SELL, WATCH, HOLD, WAIT) names what the engine sees; the **setup type** names the pattern family that produced it; the **entry zone** brackets where the setup makes sense to act on; the **stop-loss** is the single price at which the idea is wrong; the **take-profit ladder** stages exits at three levels with fixed weights (50% / 30% / 20%); and **risk:reward** compares the middle target's distance to the stop's distance.

Two honesty fields complete the card: the **reasons** list the closed-candle facts behind the state, and the **confidence basis** exposes every statistic that fed the confidence number — sample sizes included.

Nothing on the card is advice. It is the engine showing its work, so you can agree or disagree with the *reasoning*, not just the conclusion.

key_terms.definition: The engine's read: BUY_ZONE, SELL, WATCH, HOLD, or WAIT.
The pattern family that generated the state.
The price bracket where the setup is actionable.
Three staged exit levels weighted 50/30/20.
The exposed statistics behind the confidence number.

exercise.question: What does the stop-loss on a QuantGlass signal fundamentally represent?
options: A guaranteed exit price.
The price at which the setup's premise is invalidated — the idea is wrong there, so the position should not exist.
The lowest price of the year.
A fee threshold.
exercise.explanation: The stop is the falsification point of the trade idea. Execution may slip past it, but its meaning is 'beyond here, the reasoning no longer holds'.

live_apply.cta: Open any signal and locate all seven parts: state, setup, entry zone, stop, ladder, risk:reward, reasons.

visuals.title: A signal is a structured claim.

title: What the Confidence Number Means
summary: A calibrated score with visible inputs — not a probability of profit.
concept:
The confidence score (20–89) blends the setup's **trend alignment**, **volume confirmation**, **confluence**, and the **backtested record** of this setup family — with the statistics deliberately shrunk toward neutral when samples are thin. A 100%-win record over 3 trades is treated as nearly meaningless, exactly as it should be.

Two caps keep the number honest: confidence cannot exceed 62 unless the setup is **out-of-sample validated**, and it never exceeds 89 at all — certainty is not on the menu. When enough out-of-sample trades exist, the signal also shows a **coverage-guaranteed range** for the next trade's outcome, a distribution-free bound that holds with stated probability.

Read it as: *how much evidence supports this setup pattern*, never as *the chance this particular trade wins*. Single trades stay unpredictable; only the long run has statistics.

key_terms.definition: A 20–89 evidence score with all inputs exposed.
Pulling thin-sample statistics toward neutral so small samples cannot shout.
The setup's edge held on data the engine never tuned on.
A distribution-free range for the next trade's outcome at a stated probability.

exercise.question: A signal shows confidence 58 with backtest sample size 7. How should you read it?
options: A 58% chance this trade profits.
Moderate pattern evidence, heavily shrunk because seven trades is statistically almost nothing.
A guarantee of 58 ticks of profit.
The engine is 58% finished computing.
exercise.explanation: Confidence is evidence strength, not win probability — and at n=7 the shrinkage means the score leans mostly on the setup's structural quality, not its tiny track record.

live_apply.cta: Open a signal's confidence basis and find the sample sizes — notice how the score treats small ones.

visuals.title: Every input is printed in the basis.

title: Freshness and Invalidation: When a Signal Dies
summary: Signals expire two ways — by time and by price.
concept:
A signal is a statement about the market *as of its last closed candle*. It dies in two ways.

**By time**: each new closed candle re-evaluates the world; the freshness badge shows the age of the data behind what you see. Fresh (within ~2 bars) means current; delayed and stale badges mean the picture may have moved on — never act on a stale signal without refreshing.

**By price**: every signal states its **invalidation** — typically a close beyond the stop level. Once price closes there, the setup's premise is falsified, and the signal's status flips to invalidated. There is no shame in an invalidated idea; there is only error in continuing to act on one.

The discipline this teaches generalizes: every trade idea you ever hold should have a written expiry condition in both dimensions — *when* it goes stale and *where* it becomes wrong.

key_terms.definition: Fresh / delayed / stale, based on the age of the last closed candle versus the timeframe.
The stated condition (usually a close beyond the stop) that falsifies the setup.
A signal whose underlying candle is many bars old — refresh before acting.
Each closed candle triggers a fresh engine read.

exercise.question: A 1h signal shows a freshness badge of 7 hours. What is the correct response?
options: Act immediately — old signals are wine.
Refresh the data first: seven closed candles have elapsed, and the engine's current read may differ.
Double the position size to compensate.
Freshness only matters for stocks.
exercise.explanation: Seven bars of new information exist that the displayed signal has not seen. Refresh, let the engine re-evaluate, and act only on a current read.

live_apply.cta: Find the freshness badge on each signal — then refresh the corridor and watch the badges reset.

steps.text: Has price closed beyond the stop?

common_mistakes: Acting on a stale signal. A setup computed hours ago describes a market that no longer exists.
Treating invalidation as a suggestion. When the level breaks, the trade idea is dead - holding it is a new, unplanned trade.

title: Placing Your First Paper Trade
summary: From signal to simulated position, with zero dollars at risk.
concept:
Paper trading is rehearsal with real market data and fake money. In QuantGlass: open a signal, choose **Paper trade**, set a quantity, and submit. The intent is queued and filled at the next available price — including the realistic indignity of not getting the exact chart price. Your paper account tracks balance, buying power, open positions, and realized PnL.

Use the signal's own geometry: size the position so that if price hits the stop, you lose a fixed small fraction of the account (the Risk track turns this into a formula). Then — this is the rehearsal part — **manage the trade as if it were real**: respect the stop, take the ladder exits, and write down why you entered.

Everything you do here feeds the learning loop: the coaching panel watches your paper trades and surfaces the lesson you need when a habit slips.

key_terms.definition: Simulated execution against real market data.
Your queued order, filled at the next available price.
Account capacity remaining after open positions.
Profit and loss from closed positions only.

exercise.question: What is the single most valuable way to treat a paper trade?
options: As a lottery ticket — size it absurdly since it's fake.
Exactly as if it were real: planned size, respected stop, recorded reasoning.
As a way to test order buttons only.
Ignore it after entry.
exercise.explanation: Paper trading builds the habits you will keep. Practicing fantasy sizing or ignored stops rehearses exactly the behaviors that destroy real accounts.

live_apply.cta: Pick the freshest signal, place a small paper trade using its entry zone and stop, and note your reasoning in one sentence.

common_mistakes: Skipping the plan because 'it is only paper'. Paper trading exists to train the planning habit - skip it and you are practicing improvisation.
Oversizing because the money is not real. The habits formed at fake 10% risk transfer to real accounts intact.

title: After the Trade: Reading Your Results
summary: Positions, PnL, and the difference between outcome and decision.
concept:
Once filled, your position appears with an average price and a live unrealized PnL mark; closing converts it to realized PnL. The numbers are simple — the psychology is not.

Core distinction: **a good decision can lose and a bad decision can win.** One trade's outcome is dominated by noise; only across many trades does decision quality show up in the statistics. So judge each trade by *process* — was the setup valid, the size planned, the stop respected? — and judge your *method* by aggregates: win rate, average R, expectancy.

This is why QuantGlass insists on R-multiples and sample sizes everywhere: they convert a pile of individual stories into evidence. Your paper history is your first dataset. The Risk and Statistics tracks teach you to read it like an analyst rather than a gambler.

key_terms.definition: Mark-to-market profit/loss on open positions.
Locked-in result from closed positions.
Decisions are judged by their reasoning; methods by their long-run statistics.
A result expressed in units of the planned risk — the engine's native unit.

exercise.question: Your well-planned trade hit its stop for a -1R loss. What does this prove about the decision to take it?
options: The decision was bad — losses prove mistakes.
Almost nothing by itself: single outcomes are noise; the decision is judged by its process and the method by many trades.
The stop caused the loss.
You should remove stops in the future.
exercise.explanation: A -1R on a valid setup with planned size is the system working as designed. Mistake-hunting belongs at the process level, and verdicts at the sample level.

live_apply.cta: Open your paper account summary: separate in your mind which numbers are outcomes (PnL) and which are process (your sizing and stops).

title: The Anatomy of a Trading Scam
summary: Every scheme runs on the same three promises.
concept:
Trading scams are endlessly re-skinned but structurally identical. They sell some mix of three impossible promises: **guaranteed returns** ('1% daily, risk-free'), **secret knowledge** ('the indicator THEY don't want you to know'), and **effortless wealth** ('the bot trades while you sleep'). Each promise contradicts something you have already learned in this Academy: returns carry risk by definition (drawdown math), persistent public edges decay (statistical honesty), and costs plus competition make effortless profit a contradiction.

The operational pattern is also constant: **urgency** ('only 5 spots left'), **social proof you cannot verify** (screenshots, testimonials, rented Lamborghinis), and an **isolation funnel** (move you from public platforms to private Telegram/WhatsApp groups where platform moderation and witnesses disappear).

The single most protective habit: when an offer's *claimed returns* would make its seller rich without needing your money, ask why they need your money. Real edges are traded quietly, not sold loudly.

key_terms.definition: A promise that contradicts the definition of market risk — the universal scam marker.
Artificial deadlines designed to prevent verification.
Moving marks to private channels away from moderation and witnesses.
If the edge were real, selling it would be unnecessary and self-defeating.

exercise.question: An ad promises '2% per day, guaranteed, withdraw anytime'. Before any other red flag, what makes this mathematically a scam?
options: 2% is too small to bother with.
Guaranteed daily compounding at that rate would exceed the world's wealth within years — returns without risk do not exist, so the promise itself is the proof.
Withdrawals are never allowed anywhere.
Only percentages above 5% are scams.
exercise.explanation: 2% daily compounds to roughly 1,377% per year. Anyone who actually had that would never need depositors. The promise is self-refuting before you check anything else.

live_apply.cta: Revisit the drawdown lesson: the recovery math you computed there is exactly what 'risk-free returns' pretends away.

title: Fake Gurus and Signal Sellers
summary: Lifestyle marketing, unverifiable track records, and the courses funnel.
concept:
The guru business model is selling the *image* of trading success to fund a lifestyle that trading does not. Its tells: **lifestyle-first content** (cars, watches, beach laptops — never audited statements), **cherry-picked screenshots** (winners posted, losers deleted; demo accounts presented as real), **unverifiable claims** ('turned $1k into $1M' with no broker statements), and revenue that visibly comes from **courses, signals, and affiliate links** rather than trading.

**Signal sellers** deserve special caution: paying someone to tell you when to buy combines all the risk (yours) with none of the understanding (also yours). You cannot size, manage, or exit a position whose reasoning you do not know — which is why this Academy teaches you to *read the engine's reasons* rather than trust its conclusions.

The honest contrast you now own: QuantGlass shows sample sizes, out-of-sample splits, costs, and drawdowns on every claim, *because that is what honest performance reporting looks like*. Demand the same standard from any human: audited, complete, risk-adjusted, with losses included. Almost none survive the request.

key_terms.definition: Selling wealth imagery in place of verifiable performance.
Publishing winners while deleting losers — survivorship bias as a sales tool.
Charging for entry calls whose reasoning and risk stay hidden.
Complete, broker-verified performance including losses — the standard gurus avoid.

exercise.question: A guru posts 47 winning-trade screenshots this month. As a statistically-trained reader, what is the first question?
options: Which broker has the prettiest interface?
Where are the losers? A sample with no losses is curated, not earned — the same survivorship bias that fakes backtests fakes track records.
How can I subscribe fastest?
What watch is that?
exercise.explanation: You learned this as survivorship bias in the backtesting track. Screenshots are a sample you cannot audit, selected by the person selling to you.

live_apply.cta: Open any preset: losses, drawdown, and sample sizes are printed beside the wins. That completeness is what real reporting looks like.

title: Platform Scams and Phishing
summary: Fake exchanges, withdrawal traps, and credential theft.
concept:
The second scam family attacks your *access* rather than your judgment. **Fake platforms**: polished exchange/broker sites that accept deposits, display fictional profits to encourage more deposits, then block withdrawal ('pay the tax/fee first' — the **withdrawal trap**, always a second theft, never a real fee). **Phishing**: lookalike sites and emails harvesting your exchange login or seed phrase; one wrong paste and custody is gone. **Fake support**: scammers in community channels DM'ing 'help' that ends with you revealing credentials — real support never DMs first and never needs your password or seed phrase.

Defenses are mechanical, not clever: access exchanges by **bookmark only** (never search-ad links), enable **withdrawal allowlists and 2FA**, give **API keys minimum scope** (the custody lesson's rule — data-only keys for data-only apps, never withdrawal rights), and treat any 'pay to unlock your withdrawal' demand as the confession it is.

Note the QuantGlass posture you can verify: keys live in an encrypted local store, the app never asks for passwords or seed phrases, and nothing here can withdraw — by design.

key_terms.definition: Fake fees demanded to 'release' funds — a second theft, never legitimate.
Lookalike sites/messages harvesting credentials or seed phrases.
Unsolicited 'help' that ends in credential disclosure.
Reaching financial sites only through saved, verified links.

exercise.question: A 'platform' shows your balance grew to $11,400 but demands a $900 'release fee' to withdraw. What is the correct model of this situation?
options: A normal regulatory fee — pay it.
The displayed balance is fiction and the fee is a second theft attempt; no legitimate venue charges a fee to permit withdrawal of your own funds.
Negotiate the fee down to $450.
Deposit more to qualify for free withdrawals.
exercise.explanation: The number on the screen was always just pixels. Paying 'release' fees only signals you can be billed again — the correct spend is zero and the correct action is reporting.

live_apply.cta: Check your API keys screen: scopes, encryption, no withdrawal rights anywhere — verify the posture this lesson described.

title: Social Engineering and Long-Con Schemes
summary: Pig-butchering, romance funnels, and trust as the attack surface.
concept:
The most financially devastating scams attack trust, slowly. The **long-con** pattern (crudely named 'pig butchering'): a stranger initiates contact (wrong number, dating app, social media), invests weeks in friendly or romantic rapport, casually reveals their trading success, and eventually offers to 'help you invest' — on a platform they control. Small withdrawals are honored early to manufacture trust; the theft happens after the victim escalates to life savings. Losses in this category are routinely catastrophic precisely because the relationship feels real.

Structural defenses, because in-the-moment judgment is exactly what the con disables: **the channel rule** — anyone who initiates contact AND eventually steers to investing is presumed hostile, no matter how long the rapport took; **the platform rule** — never use a venue introduced by an online acquaintance; **the verification rule** — discuss any new 'opportunity' with someone offline who loves you and gains nothing; and **the exit rule** — early 'successful withdrawals' prove nothing, since honoring small exits is the scheme's working capital.

If this has happened to you or someone near you: it is organized industrial crime, not gullibility — report it, and never pay 'recovery services' (the follow-up scam that targets victims twice).

key_terms.definition: Weeks of manufactured rapport before the financial ask.
Honored small withdrawals used to unlock larger deposits.
Initiated-contact + investment-steering = presumed hostile.
Fake services that charge victims to 'retrieve' stolen funds.

exercise.question: An online friend of two months, who has never asked for anything, mentions their uncle's trading platform and offers to walk you through it. Which single fact dominates the risk assessment?
options: Two months is long enough to establish trust.
They initiated the original contact and the path now leads to an investment platform they introduced — the full long-con signature, regardless of how genuine the rapport feels.
Uncles are generally trustworthy.
The platform's website looks professional.
exercise.explanation: The con's entire design is that the relationship feels real and unhurried. Pattern beats feeling: initiated contact plus steered investing is the signature, and the only safe response is the channel rule.

live_apply.cta: Share the channel rule with one person you know who is new to investing — defense spreads by being said out loud.

title: The Scam Checklist
summary: Ten questions that filter nearly everything.
concept:
Compressed from this track, a checklist to run against any opportunity — each 'yes' is a red flag, three is a verdict:

1. Are returns **guaranteed** or 'risk-free'?
2. Is there **urgency** (deadlines, limited spots)?
3. Did **they contact you** first?
4. Are you being moved to a **private channel**?
5. Is the track record **unverifiable** (screenshots, no audited statements)?
6. Does the seller earn from **fees/courses/referrals** rather than trading?
7. Must you use **their platform** or pay to withdraw?
8. Do they want **credentials, seed phrases, or remote access**?
9. Is the strategy **secret** ('can't reveal how it works')?
10. Would the claimed returns make selling **unnecessary** if real?

Notice the pattern in what survives this filter: boring things. Self-custodied learning, audited public data, tools that show their work, returns claimed in honest R-multiples with drawdowns attached. The Academy's closing irony is intentional: everything that made the engine trustworthy — visible reasoning, sample sizes, out-of-sample honesty, costs included, no promises — is the exact inverse of every red flag above. You now know what honesty looks like in this domain. Demand it everywhere.

key_terms.definition: One flag is suspicion; three is a verdict.
The dividing line between marketing and evidence.
What passes the filter: audited, transparent, promise-free.
Every engine transparency feature mirrors a scam red flag.

exercise.question: Which property do essentially ALL offers that survive the ten-question checklist share?
options: Higher promised returns than the scams.
Verifiable transparency: auditable claims, visible reasoning, included losses, and no dependence on urgency or secrecy.
Celebrity endorsements.
Private Telegram access.
exercise.explanation: The filter doesn't find hidden gems — it finds honesty. Legitimate tools and teachers survive because they show their work; everything else needs the flags to function.

live_apply.cta: Run the checklist against QuantGlass itself, item by item — the engine should pass precisely because every claim on screen is auditable.

common_mistakes: Assuming intelligence is protection. Scams are engineered for smart, confident people - the checklist works precisely because it does not rely on judgment in the moment.
Checking the list only for strangers. Friends and family forward scams they believe in; the list applies to the message, not the messenger.
